L1.area: Platform-specific memory allocation and resource management

L2.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in a memory allocation routine where a user-controlled size parameter is added to a fixed internal structure size without overflow checking. A specially crafted large size value causes the arithmetic to wrap around, resulting in a smaller-than-expected heap allocation. This leads to a heap-based buffer overflow, potentially allowing denial of service or other impacts.

L3.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the Windows kernel-mode memory allocation routine where a user-controlled size parameter is added to a fixed internal structure size without overflow checking. When deployed as a Windows kernel driver, a specially crafted large size value causes the arithmetic to wrap around, resulting in a smaller-than-expected kernel pool allocation and a subsequent heap-based buffer overflow, potentially allowing denial of service or privilege escalation.

ground_truth.reason: The cs_winkernel_malloc function adds user-supplied size to sizeof(CS_WINKERNEL_MEMBLOCK) without overflow checking, allowing a crafted large value to wrap around and allocate a smaller-than-expected buffer, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow.
